title: Observation of Electrically Tunable Chirality Inversion in a Slow-Light Waveguide
authors: Xuchao Chen, Savvas Germanis, Nicholas J. Martin, Hamidreza Siampour, René Dost, Dominic J. Hallett, Ian Farrer, Akshay Kumar Verma, Maurice S. Skolnick, Luke R. Wilson, A. Mark Fox
abstract: We identify chiral inversion points in slow-light, glide-plane-symmetric, photonic-crystal waveguides, defined as fixed locations where the local optical chirality changes sign over a narrow wavelength range. We experimentally access this behaviour using a waveguide-embedded InAs/InGaAs quantum dot. The slow-light spectral region is determined from time-integrated and time-resolved photoluminescence, and the dot exciton is electrically tuned across the slow-light bandwidth via the quantum-confined Stark effect. As the emission wavelength is swept through the slow-light region, the directional emission contrast shows a strong wavelength dependence and a sign reversal, consistent with the identified chiral inversion point. Numerical simulations attribute the switching primarily to the pronounced spectral variation of the local optical chirality for emitters displaced from the waveguide center. These results demonstrate on-demand electrical switching of chiral light-matter coupling in nanophotonic waveguides and enable tunable chiral interfaces for integrated quantum photonic devices.
summary: This research reports the experimental observation of electrically tunable chirality inversion in a slow-light photonic-crystal waveguide. By embedding a quantum dot and tuning its emission wavelength, the researchers measured a sign reversal in the directional emission contrast. This confirms that the local optical chirality can be actively controlled in situ, enabling the development of advanced, tunable chiral interfaces for integrated quantum photonics.
main_problem: The study aims to observe and demonstrate electrically tunable chirality inversion within a slow-light photonic-crystal waveguide structure.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrated a sign reversal in the directional emission contrast, confirming that the local optical chirality can be actively switched on-demand by tuning the quantum dot emission wavelength across the slow-light band.
method: The experiment uses time-resolved photoluminescence measurements on a waveguide-embedded quantum dot, while numerical simulations (FDTD/GME) are used to model the local optical chirality and coupling efficiency.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a glide-plane photonic-crystal waveguide (GPW) containing an embedded InAs/InGaAs quantum dot (QD). The system operates in the slow-light regime, allowing electrical tuning of the QD exciton via the quantum-confined Stark effect.
key_observables: Directional emission contrast (D), local optical chirality (quantified by the Stokes parameter $S_3$), Purcell factor ($F_P$), and radiative coupling efficiency ($ eta$).
important_parameters: Slow-light spectral region (identified by minimum exciton lifetime, e.g., $\sim 760$ ps at 914.3 nm); off-center emitter positions ($y/a > 0.5$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the sign reversal is primarily due to the intrinsic wavelength dependence of the local chirality ($S_3$), rather than just spectral shaping effects from Purcell enhancement.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device schematics, spatial maps of $F_P$ and $S_3$, and critically, the measured directional contrast $D$ showing a clear sign reversal as the emission wavelength is tuned across the slow-light region.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical system and the theoretical framework (calculating $F_P$, $ eta$, and $S_3$). It then presents experimental measurements showing the directional contrast sign reversal, attributing this effect to the wavelength dependence of the local chirality, and finally distinguishing this effect from minor spectral shaping contributions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems because it demonstrates active, electrical control over fundamental optical properties (chirality) within a solid-state nanophotonic platform, enabling tunable chiral interfaces for quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Device schematic layout and working principle. A QD located in the active region of the waveguide is optically excited, and the emitted photons are guided toward grating out-couplers at both ends for photoluminescence collection. (b) An SEM image showing a part of the active region of GPW device, together with a schematic of the p-i-n diode structure. The scale bar represents 500 nm. (c) Simulated band structure of the central GPW region and the extracted group index ng. Five sampling wavelengths are highlighted on the dispersion curve in an increasing order and are used for (d)-(e). The light cone is shaded in purple.
